content: Kill criteria are the specific, measurable conditions you define before starting a project that will trigger an exit — not vague feelings of discomfort, but concrete observable signals. Duke argues these must be set in advance, before emotional investment clouds judgment, because once you are in the middle of a bet the same cognitive distortions that made you start will keep you going. The power of kill criteria is precisely that they remove the decision from the emotionally compromised future self and hand it to the clearer-headed present self.
implication: Before launching any initiative, product, or strategy, define in writing the specific metrics, timelines, or conditions that would trigger a stop. Treat that document as a contract with your future self — not a suggestion.
source_ref: Quit: The Power of Knowing When to Walk Away — Annie Duke (2022)

content: A pre-mortem works by projecting yourself into a future where the project has already failed and then asking what went wrong. Unlike forward-looking planning, which is dominated by optimism and confirmation bias, the pre-mortem forcibly activates your ability to imagine failure — surfaces risks that enthusiasm systematically hides. Duke positions this technique as an antidote to the natural human tendency to fall in love with a plan the moment you've made it.
implication: Before committing resources to any major decision, run a structured pre-mortem session. Explicitly asking 'it's a year from now and this failed — what happened?' will surface risks that no amount of optimistic planning will reveal.
source_ref: Thinking in Bets: Making Smarter Decisions When You Don't Have All the Facts — Annie Duke (2018)

content: Pre-commitment contracts are agreements you make with yourself — or with an accountability group — in advance of being tested, specifying exactly what you will do under defined future conditions. Duke draws on the analogy of Ulysses tying himself to the mast: he knew his future self would be compromised by the Sirens, so his present self removed the choice. The same logic applies to business and life decisions — your future self in the emotional fog of the moment cannot be trusted to follow rational rules without structural enforcement.
implication: High-stakes decisions require structural enforcement, not willpower. Build pre-commitment mechanisms — written contracts, accountability partners, scheduled review gates — that your future emotional state cannot easily override.
source_ref: Thinking in Bets: Making Smarter Decisions When You Don't Have All the Facts — Annie Duke (2018)

content: Duke observes that the optimal time to set exit conditions is the same moment you decide to start — when you are most clear-headed, most objective, and least emotionally tied to outcomes. Waiting until problems emerge to define what constitutes a problem is too late; by then, motivated reasoning has already recruited your analytical faculties in defense of continuing. The kill criteria set at the beginning are the only ones that will be honest.
implication: Treat the moment of commitment as a mandatory dual-task: decide to start AND define what would make you stop. These two decisions belong together, not separated by weeks or months of emotional investment.
source_ref: Quit by Annie Duke | Core Message — YouTube

content: One of Duke's central arguments in Quit is that the signals telling you to exit a failing path are almost always visible long before most people act on them — the problem is not a lack of information but a systematically corrupted interpretation of that information. Sunk costs, identity investment, and social pressure all conspire to reframe bad signals as temporary setbacks requiring more persistence. Pre-defined kill criteria bypass this interpretive corruption by converting a judgment call into a rule.
implication: The question is rarely 'do we have enough information to know this isn't working?' — it is usually 'are we interpreting that information honestly?' Pre-defined criteria remove the interpretation problem by making the decision automatic.
source_ref: Quit: The Power of Knowing When to Walk Away — Annie Duke (2022)

content: Duke describes the pre-mortem as an exercise that deliberately exploits the human brain's natural capacity for narrative — instead of asking 'what could go wrong?' in the abstract, you tell yourself the story of a failure that has already happened. This narrative framing unlocks a richer, more specific set of failure modes because people are much better at explaining the past than predicting the future. The technique essentially tricks your brain into lending its hindsight machinery to a foresight problem.
implication: Frame your risk-assessment exercises as post-mortems set in the future, not as speculation. 'The project failed — tell me the story of why' produces far richer risk maps than 'what are the possible risks?'
source_ref: Thinking in Bets | Annie Duke | Talks at Google — YouTube

content: Duke points out that most organizations run post-mortems — retrospective analyses after a project has failed — but almost none run pre-mortems before committing. This sequencing error is catastrophic: once resources are deployed and egos are attached to outcomes, the post-mortem becomes a political exercise in blame distribution rather than honest risk identification. Moving the mortem to before the commitment changes the entire incentive structure of the conversation.
implication: Institutionalize the pre-mortem as a mandatory gate before major resource commitments. The absence of political stakes at the beginning of a project is exactly the condition that makes honest risk assessment possible.
source_ref: Author Talks: What poker pro Annie Duke can teach you about quitting on time — YouTube

content: Duke emphasizes that kill criteria must be specific and observable, not vague and feeling-based. A kill criterion like 'if we're not feeling good about the direction' is not a kill criterion — it is an invitation for motivated reasoning to declare perpetual optimism. Effective kill criteria look like: 'if metric X has not reached Y by date Z, we stop.' The specificity is not bureaucratic pedantry; it is the entire mechanism by which the criterion does its work.
implication: Review your current project exit conditions. If any of them could be interpreted differently depending on how much you want to continue, they are not kill criteria — they are escape hatches for motivated reasoning. Rewrite them with numbers and dates.
source_ref: Quit: The Power of Knowing When to Walk Away — Annie Duke (2022)

content: Duke explains that one of the most powerful properties of pre-commitment is that it converts a recurring judgment call — should I keep going? — into a one-time decision made under optimal conditions. Every time you face that recurring question without pre-commitment, you must overcome the full weight of sunk costs, social pressure, and identity protection. Pre-commitment collapses thousands of future corrupted decisions into one clean decision made before any of those distortions exist.
implication: Recognize that 'should I continue?' is not one decision — it is a decision you will make thousands of times, most of them under terrible cognitive conditions. A single well-structured pre-commitment is worth more than any number of in-the-moment reviews.
source_ref: Quit: The Power of Knowing When to Walk Away — Annie Duke (2022)

content: In her TEDxGeorgetown talk, Duke uses poker as a living laboratory for pre-commitment thinking: professional players set explicit loss limits before sitting down, not because they lack confidence in their skill, but precisely because they know that losses distort judgment and a pre-set limit removes the corrupted in-session decision entirely. The same principle scales directly to business — the stop-loss is not pessimism, it is rational protection against the decision-making degradation that follows emotional arousal.
implication: Define your loss limit before you sit down at the table — whether that table is a new product line, a market expansion, or a strategic partnership. The poker player's stop-loss is one of the most transferable risk management tools from the game.
source_ref: Risk Schmisk | Annie Duke | TEDxGeorgetown — YouTube

content: Duke argues that the cultural narrative around persistence — the idea that successful people never quit — creates a dangerous asymmetry in how we interpret quitting signals. When things get hard, we interpret difficulty as a reason to persist (grit narrative) rather than as potential evidence that the underlying thesis is broken. Pre-mortems and kill criteria are partly a corrective to this narrative bias: they establish in advance what difficulty looks like versus what a broken thesis looks like, so the two can be distinguished in the moment.
implication: Build into your kill criteria the explicit distinction between 'this is hard because execution is difficult' and 'this is hard because the fundamental premise is wrong.' These require completely different responses — persistence and pivot — and conflating them is one of the most expensive errors in strategy.
source_ref: Quit: The Power of Knowing When to Walk Away — Annie Duke (2022)

content: Duke notes that having an outside accountability partner review your kill criteria dramatically increases the probability you will actually honor them. Because our own motivated reasoning operates invisibly to us — we don't feel ourselves rationalizing — a committed external reviewer who knows the original criteria can identify when we are reinterpreting them in self-serving ways. The accountability partner's job is not to make the decision but to hold up a mirror to the reasoning.
implication: Assign a designated skeptic — a board member, advisor, or peer — whose explicit role is to compare your current reasoning against your original kill criteria. Their value is not judgment but memory: they remember what you agreed to before you wanted to change it.
source_ref: Annie Duke on Knowing When to Quit — YouTube

content: Duke describes what she calls 'resulting' as particularly dangerous in the context of kill criteria: if a project is going well, people assume the underlying thesis is sound and relax their exit monitoring. But good short-term results can easily be luck — and luck eventually reverts. A robust kill criteria system monitors the underlying drivers of success, not just the surface outcomes, because only the drivers can distinguish skill-based progress from luck-based noise.
implication: Design kill criteria around leading indicators and underlying mechanics, not just lagging outcome metrics. If your criteria would keep you in a lucky-but-broken business, they aren't doing their job.
source_ref: Thinking in Bets: Making Smarter Decisions When You Don't Have All the Facts — Annie Duke (2018)

content: Duke frames the pre-mortem as a tool for reducing overconfidence at the moment of maximum optimism — which is exactly when a commitment decision is being made. Research she references shows that imagining a specific failure mechanism is far more effective at surfacing genuine risks than general probability assessments, because it bypasses the planning fallacy and forces engagement with concrete causal paths. The technique works because specificity defeats optimism in ways that abstract risk percentages cannot.
implication: Don't ask your team 'how likely is this to fail?' — ask them to write a detailed two-paragraph story of how it failed. Specificity is the mechanism; the probability number is secondary.
source_ref: The Reason Smart People Make Wrong Decisions | Annie Duke — YouTube

content: Duke observes that the muggy middle — the messy, ambiguous phase of any project — is precisely when kill criteria are most valuable and most difficult to honor. At this stage, sunk costs are high, the end is theoretically within reach, and every signal of failure can be reinterpreted as a temporary setback. Pre-defined criteria are the only protection against this specific cognitive environment because they were written by someone who hadn't yet experienced any of those distortions.
implication: Expect maximum pressure on your kill criteria in the messy middle — not at the beginning and not at the clear end. Design your criteria review process to be most rigorous precisely when internal pressure to override them will be greatest.
source_ref: Quit: The Power of Knowing When to Walk Away — Annie Duke (2022)

content: Duke draws a sharp distinction between a pre-mortem — which surfaces risk before commitment — and a post-mortem, which analyzes failure after it has occurred. The post-mortem has one fatal flaw: the outcome is already known, which means hindsight bias will corrupt every causal inference the team draws. Pre-mortems are conducted under genuine uncertainty, which is the only condition in which honest risk identification is psychologically possible.
implication: Run post-mortems for learning, but don't rely on them for risk management — hindsight bias guarantees that retrospective risk analysis will be systematically distorted. Only pre-mortems operate in the cognitive conditions where honest risk identification is achievable.
source_ref: Thinking in Bets for Engineers 🎲 — with Annie Duke — YouTube

content: Duke argues that pre-commitment to quit conditions is especially critical when identity is invested in a venture. When a project becomes part of who you are — your startup, your thesis, your public bet — quitting feels like self-annihilation, not rational updating. Kill criteria set before identity attachment forms are the last line of defense, because once identity is fused with the project, even awareness of sunk cost fallacy is insufficient to override the self-protective instinct to continue.
implication: The higher the identity investment in a venture, the more critical it is to set kill criteria before that investment forms. By the time a project defines your professional identity, you have lost the cognitive independence needed to set honest exit conditions.
source_ref: Quit: The Power of Knowing When to Walk Away — Annie Duke (2022)

content: Duke uses the concept of 'backcasting' alongside pre-mortems: where a pre-mortem imagines failure and asks what caused it, backcasting imagines success and asks what conditions made it possible. Together, the two techniques bracket the decision space — one maps the failure pathways, the other maps the conditions required for success — and the overlap between them defines the kill criteria. If the success conditions are not being met, the failure pathways become the likely story.
implication: Pair every pre-mortem with a backcast: define both what success looks like mechanistically and what failure looks like narratively. The gap between the two defines exactly what you need to monitor — and what crossing into failure territory looks like.
source_ref: How to Decide: Simple Tools for Making Better Choices — Annie Duke (2020)

content: Duke emphasizes that pre-commitment contracts are not expressions of pessimism or lack of conviction — they are expressions of epistemic honesty about the limits of your own future rationality. The person who refuses to set kill criteria because they are 'fully committed' is not demonstrating confidence; they are demonstrating unawareness of how cognitive biases will compromise their future judgment under pressure. The most sophisticated decision-makers are the most willing to pre-commit.
implication: Reframe pre-commitment and kill criteria from signs of doubt to signs of decision-making maturity. The leader who refuses to define exit conditions isn't displaying conviction — they're displaying ignorance of their own cognitive limitations.
source_ref: Former Poker Player Lays Out Life Rules To Know When It's Time To Quit — YouTube

content: In discussing her book Quit, Duke points out that setting kill criteria creates a valuable side effect beyond just enabling timely exits: it forces you to articulate your underlying thesis with enough precision that it can actually be falsified. Projects without kill criteria typically lack a clear falsifiable thesis — they are sustained by narrative momentum and social proof rather than evidence. The discipline of writing kill criteria is simultaneously a discipline of strategic clarity.
implication: If you cannot write a kill criterion for your project, you may not have a clear enough thesis to justify starting it. The inability to define failure conditions is often a symptom of a strategy built on hope rather than hypothesis.
source_ref: Why the Best Quitters Win! | feat. Annie Duke | Ideas Lab 8 — YouTube

content: Duke notes that decision groups and accountability pods are the institutional version of pre-commitment — a structure that makes it socially difficult to abandon agreed-upon kill criteria unilaterally. Because motivated reasoning operates inside the individual mind, external social accountability is one of the few reliable mechanisms to catch it. Sharing your kill criteria publicly with a trusted group converts a private internal commitment into a social contract, which is dramatically harder to rationalize away.
implication: Publicize your kill criteria within a trusted accountability group before you start. The social cost of unilaterally renegotiating them later is one of the few forces strong enough to counteract motivated reasoning in the moment of maximum pressure.
source_ref: Thinking in Bets: Making Smarter Decisions When You Don't Have All the Facts — Annie Duke (2018)

content: Duke highlights that one of the most common failures of kill criteria in practice is that people write them and then renegotiate them the moment they are triggered. This 'moving the goalposts' behavior is not a flaw in the criteria — it is motivated reasoning finding its most natural outlet. The remedy is to require any revision of kill criteria to go through the same external review process as the original criteria, making renegotiation as deliberate and costly as the original commitment.
implication: Treat any proposed revision to kill criteria with extreme suspicion and require it to pass the same review bar as the original. The impulse to renegotiate kill criteria is almost always motivated reasoning, not new information.
source_ref: Quit: The Power of Knowing When to Walk Away — Annie Duke (2022)

content: Duke connects pre-mortems directly to the concept of opportunity cost: the pre-mortem isn't only asking 'why did this specific project fail?' — it is also asking 'what did we fail to do because we were committed to this project?' Making the opportunity cost of persistence visible at the outset changes the entire framing of the continue/quit decision. Quitting isn't just avoiding further loss in the current path; it is recovering the capacity to pursue a better one.
implication: Include opportunity cost explicitly in your pre-mortem. Ask not just 'what went wrong with this?' but also 'what did we not pursue while committed to this?' The second question is often the more painful and more honest answer.
source_ref: Quit: The Power of Knowing When to Walk Away — Annie Duke (2022)
